threats.title: PermissionValidator Out-of-Bounds Calldata Read Bypasses Session Key Parameter Rules
threats.hypothesis_description: LibBytes.readBytes32 performs no bounds checking on calldata reads. In PermissionValidator.validatePermission, rule.offset can exceed call.data.length, causing reads into adjacent ABI-encoded calldata. The target contract receives only call.data (which is shorter), so it zero-pads the missing bytes. This mismatch allows a session key holder to craft short call.data where the permission validator reads attacker-controlled adjacent calldata satisfying the rule, while the target contract processes different (zero-padded) parameters, effectively bypassing permission enforcement.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect in the permission validation path. Session keys are explicitly constrained by permissions, and this bypass would allow session keys to call target contracts with parameters outside their authorized range, potentially draining wallet funds or calling unauthorized functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bytes32 value,) = call.data.readBytes32(rule.offset) — no check that rule.offset + 32 <= call.data.length; LibBytes.readBytes32 uses calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset)) which reads beyond slice bounds into adjacent calldata

preconditions: Wallet has a session key configured with at least one explicit permission containing parameter rules
SessionManager is registered as a Sapient signer in the wallet configuration
Session key holder crafts a payload where call.data is shorter than rule.offset + 32

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Session key crafts call.data shorter than rule.offset, causing OOB read into adjacent ABI-encoded payload data
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action required — session key holder exploits independently
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a session key is active with parameter rules

operational_questions: Can the session key holder precisely control the ABI-encoded calldata layout to place desired bytes at the OOB read position?
Which specific permission rule types (EQUAL, LESS_THAN_OR_EQUAL, etc.) are most exploitable?
Does the Solidity ABI encoder produce deterministic layout that the attacker can predict?
validation_requirements: Construct a PoC with short call.data where OOB read returns value satisfying the rule
Verify target contract receives different parameters than what validator checked
Test with cumulative rules to confirm usage tracking is also bypassed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Session key holder identifies permission rules with specific offset values
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft call.data with only function selector (4 bytes), omitting parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Arrange subsequent calls in the payload so their ABI encoding places bytes satisfying the rule at the OOB read position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Target contract receives short calldata, interprets missing params as zero, executes unintended action
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single session key holder, no coordination needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Session key bypasses all parameter-level permission restrictions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Session key can call authorized targets with arbitrary parameters outside permitted range
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All explicit session calls with parameter rules

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of ABI encoding layout and calldata positioning
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time while session is active

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Session transactions with unusually short call.data for calls that normally require parameters
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Parameter values in executed calls differing from permission rule constraints
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Session key calling targets with zero-padded parameters that bypass intended restrictions

threats.title: Unchecked Weight Overflow in recoverBranch Causes Signature Rejection (DoS)
threats.hypothesis_description: BaseSig.recoverBranch runs entirely in an unchecked block. When FLAG_SUBDIGEST or FLAG_SIGNATURE_ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST matches, weight is set to type(uint256).max. Any subsequent signer in the same Merkle branch that adds weight (via FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH, FLAG_SIGNATURE_ERC1271, FLAG_SIGNATURE_SAPIENT, etc.) causes uint256 overflow, wrapping weight to a small value (e.g., type(uint256).max + 1 = 0). This makes weight < threshold, causing legitimate transactions to be rejected.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect in the core signature recovery logic. Wallet configurations that include a hardcoded subdigest alongside other signers at the same Merkle tree level will break when using the subdigest to authenticate, as providing any additional signer's signature in the same branch triggers the overflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { ... weight = type(uint256).max; ... weight += addrWeight; ... } — the entire recoverBranch is unchecked; FLAG_SUBDIGEST sets weight to max, then FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH adds weight causing overflow

preconditions: Wallet configuration includes a hardcoded subdigest leaf in the same Merkle branch as other signers with non-zero weight
Signature includes both the matching subdigest AND a weight-contributing signer flag for another item in the same branch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Valid subdigest match followed by any weight addition overflows to near-zero
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Affects wallet owner's ability to use subdigest authentication
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for any config with subdigest + other signers in same branch

operational_questions: How common are wallet configs with subdigest leaves alongside other signers at the same tree level?
Can off-chain SDK prevent this configuration pattern?
Does FLAG_SIGNATURE_ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST have the same issue?
validation_requirements: Create wallet config with subdigest + signer in same branch
Construct signature with matching subdigest + valid signer sig in same branch
Verify weight overflows and transaction is rejected

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify wallet configs using hardcoded subdigest with other signers in same Merkle branch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any attempt to use subdigest auth while also providing signatures for co-located signers triggers overflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction is rejected despite valid subdigest match, preventing wallet operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wallet owner must restructure config to isolate subdigest in its own branch
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this is a correctness bug affecting wallet availability

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None — this is a DoS, not privilege escalation
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Wallet cannot execute transactions using subdigest auth path
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires config update to restructure Merkle tree (need alternative auth path)

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time subdigest auth is attempted with co-located signers

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transaction attempts from wallets with subdigest configs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Consistent signature validation failures despite valid subdigest match
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Wallets unable to use pre-authorized subdigest operations

threats.title: ecrecover address(0) Returns Grant Weight Without Valid Key in BaseSig
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseSig.recoverBranch, FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH and FLAG_SIGNATURE_ETH_SIGN do not validate that ecrecover returns a non-zero address. When ecrecover fails (invalid signature), it returns address(0), yet the weight is unconditionally added and a leaf node for address(0) is computed. If a wallet configuration includes address(0) as a signer leaf (via misconfiguration), any invalid ECDSA signature would count as a valid signature from address(0), granting its full weight without possession of any private key.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a missing validation check in the core signature recovery. While configurations should not include address(0), the contract does not prevent it, and any misconfiguration could lead to complete wallet takeover without requiring any cryptographic material.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address addr = ecrecover(_opHash, v, r, s); weight += addrWeight; — no check for addr == address(0) before adding weight

preconditions: Wallet configuration includes address(0) as a signer with non-zero weight in its Merkle tree
address(0)'s weight meets or contributes sufficiently to meet threshold

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Invalid ECDSA signatures produce address(0) via ecrecover, weight added unconditionally
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires misconfigured wallet config including address(0)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once address(0) is in config

operational_questions: Can the off-chain SDK or deployment tools prevent address(0) from being included in signer configs?
Are there any wallet deployments with address(0) in their config trees?
Does the Merkle tree construction make accidental inclusion of address(0) possible?
validation_requirements: Deploy wallet with address(0) as a signer with sufficient weight
Submit transaction with invalid ECDSA signature
Verify ecrecover returns address(0) and weight is added, allowing authentication

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify wallet with address(0) in signer config (or trick owner into creating one)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft signature with r=0, s=0 compact format — ecrecover returns address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Weight is added for address(0), imageHash matches config with address(0) leaf
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If weight meets threshold, attacker gains full wallet control
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — attacker needs only the misconfigured wallet address

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of ecrecover and ECDSA

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Wallet configs with address(0) in Merkle tree leaves
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Transaction execution with ecrecover returning address(0)
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Unauthorized transactions from wallets without valid signers

threats.title: Recovery ecrecover address(0) Allows Unauthorized Payload Queuing
threats.hypothesis_description: Recovery.isValidSignature does not check for ecrecover returning address(0). When _signer is address(0) and a 64-byte invalid signature is provided, ecrecover returns address(0), and addr == _signer evaluates to true. This allows anyone to queue recovery payloads for any wallet using _signer = address(0). If the wallet's recovery configuration includes address(0) as a recovery leaf, the queued payload becomes executable after the timelock.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Recovery contract's signature validation has the same ecrecover address(0) issue as BaseSig. Combined with a recovery config that includes address(0), an attacker can queue malicious config update payloads without any valid key.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address addr = ecrecover(rPayloadHash, v, r, s); if (addr == _signer) { return true; } — no check for addr == address(0); when _signer is address(0), any invalid 64-byte signature passes

preconditions: Wallet has Recovery contract as a sapient signer in its configuration
Recovery configuration includes address(0) as a recovery signer leaf
Attacker calls queuePayload with _signer = address(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: queuePayload called with _signer = address(0) and any 64-byte invalid signature succeeds
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires recovery config with address(0) as signer

operational_questions: Can recovery configs be constructed with address(0) as a signer?
Is there any on-chain validation preventing address(0) in recovery leaves?
How many deployed wallets might have recovery configs vulnerable to this?
validation_requirements: Deploy wallet with recovery config including address(0) signer
Call queuePayload with _signer=address(0) and invalid 64-byte signature
Wait for timelock and verify recovery payload becomes executable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify wallet with address(0) in recovery config or create one via social engineering
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Execute the recovery to update wallet config to attacker's control

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full wallet takeover via config update
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must update config before timelock expires

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Recovery payloads queued without valid signatures
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Unauthorized recovery attempts targeting wallets with address(0) in config

threats.title: Recovery Payloads Cannot Be Cancelled — Compromised Signer Forces Timelock Race
threats.hypothesis_description: The Recovery contract has no mechanism to cancel queued payloads. Once a recovery payload is queued via queuePayload, the timestampForQueuedPayload entry is permanent (write-once, never cleared). If a recovery signer's key is compromised, the attacker queues a malicious config update. The wallet owner cannot cancel this payload; their only defense is to update the wallet configuration to invalidate the recovery signer BEFORE the timelock expires. If the owner fails to act within the timelock window, the attacker can execute the recovery.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing cancellation functionality is a design gap that creates operational risk. Recovery timelocks are typically accompanied by cancellation mechanisms to handle key compromise scenarios.

proof_of_signal.pattern: timestampForQueuedPayload[_wallet][_signer][payloadHash] = block.timestamp; — write-once, no cancel/clear function exists in the contract

preconditions: Wallet uses Recovery contract as a sapient signer
Recovery signer's key is compromised
Attacker queues a malicious config update payload

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No cancel/delete/invalidate function exists for queued recovery payloads
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner must update config before timelock expires — sole defense
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: requiredDeltaTime duration between queue and execution

operational_questions: What is the typical requiredDeltaTime used in production recovery configs?
Is the wallet owner notified of queued recovery payloads?
Can the owner reliably update config within the timelock window?
validation_requirements: Verify no cancellation mechanism exists in Recovery contract
Test scenario: queue payload, then attempt to prevent execution
Confirm only config update (removing signer) prevents execution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromise recovery signer key (phishing, key theft, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Queue malicious config update payload targeting the wallet
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Execute recovery to take over wallet configuration
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires off-chain key compromise — LOW realism for on-chain-only

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full wallet config takeover if owner doesn't respond in time
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Race condition between attacker execution and owner config update
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner must update config before timelock expiry

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Recovery payloads queued by known-compromised signers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple recovery attempts in rapid succession

threats.title: Static Signature Caller Restriction Fails When Used Via ERC4337 Path
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseAuth.signatureValidation, static signatures with a non-zero caller restriction check addr != msg.sender. When validateUserOp calls this.isValidSignature(userOpHash, userOp.signature), the msg.sender for isValidSignature becomes address(this) (the wallet itself), not the original EntryPoint or the UserOperation sender. A static signature restricted to a specific external caller (e.g., a relayer address) would fail validation via the ERC4337 path because msg.sender is the wallet, not the intended caller.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a functional inconsistency between direct signature validation and ERC4337-mediated validation. Static signatures with caller restrictions cannot be used via ERC4337, potentially causing unexpected transaction failures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: this.isValidSignature(userOpHash, userOp.signature) — external self-call changes msg.sender to address(this), breaking static signature caller checks

preconditions: Wallet has a static signature set with a non-zero caller address restriction
Transaction is submitted via ERC4337 EntryPoint (validateUserOp path)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: this.isValidSignature makes msg.sender = address(this), bypassing intended caller restriction
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner sets static signature with caller restriction
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any ERC4337 transaction using static signature with caller restriction

operational_questions: Are static signatures with caller restrictions commonly used in production?
Is this a known design limitation or an unintended interaction?
Could an attacker set a static signature restricted to address(this) to create a backdoor only usable via ERC4337?
validation_requirements: Set static signature with caller = specific address
Submit via ERC4337 and verify rejection
Test with caller = address(0) and caller = address(this)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wallet owner sets static signature restricted to specific relayer address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: ERC4337 submission causes this.isValidSignature call where msg.sender = address(this)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Static signature validation fails because msg.sender != expected caller
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction fails unexpectedly (DoS), or attacker exploits by setting addr=address(this)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for DoS; attacker needs wallet access for backdoor

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Potential backdoor if static sig restricted to address(this) is set
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Static signatures with caller restrictions fail via ERC4337
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires removing or updating the static signature

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of ERC4337 flow and msg.sender semantics
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time static signature with caller restriction is used via ERC4337

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed validateUserOp calls with static signature errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Static signatures that work via direct execute but fail via ERC4337

threats.title: Session Call Hash Loses Chain Binding When noChainId Is Set, Enabling Cross-Chain Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: SessionSig.hashCallWithReplayProtection uses payload.noChainId to determine whether to include block.chainid in the per-call hash. When noChainId is true, the hash uses 0 instead of the actual chain ID. Since the session key signs per-call hashes, and noChainId is set by the outer signature flag (not controlled by the session key), session call signatures become replayable across chains. An attacker who captures a session transaction on one chain can replay it on another chain where the same wallet exists with the same session configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The noChainId flag was designed for cross-chain wallet operations but creates replay risk for session-bounded calls. Session keys are typically granted limited permissions, and cross-chain replay could allow unauthorized operations on chains the session key was not intended to operate on.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payload.noChainId ? 0 : block.chainid — when noChainId is true, session call hash excludes chainid, enabling cross-chain replay of session signatures

preconditions: Wallet uses session keys with the SessionManager
Outer signature uses noChainId = true
Same wallet address exists on multiple chains with same session configuration
Same nonce space and nonce value are available on both chains

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Session signatures without chain binding can be replayed on other chains
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner or signer enables noChainId via outer signature flag
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While session is active on both chains

operational_questions: How often is noChainId used in production session transactions?
Does the SessionManager enforce chainId check separately from the session call hash?
Can the nonce space prevent cross-chain replay?
validation_requirements: Create session transaction with noChainId on chain A
Replay the session signature on chain B with same wallet
Verify whether nonce check prevents or allows the replay

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe session transaction with noChainId on chain A
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Construct equivalent transaction on chain B with same payload and session signatures
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit replayed transaction if nonce space allows
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Session calls execute on chain B without session key's intent
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires observing confirmed transaction on chain A

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Session key operations replayed across chains
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must revoke session or consume nonces to prevent replay

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of cross-chain replay and session encoding
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before nonce is consumed on target chain

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Session transactions with identical signatures on multiple chains
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected session operations on chains where session was not intended
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same payload/signature appearing on multiple chains

threats.title: Hooks Fallback Delegates to Registered Hook Without Caller Authentication
threats.hypothesis_description: The Hooks.fallback function performs a delegatecall to the registered hook implementation for any matching selector, regardless of who the caller is. Any external address can trigger hook execution by calling the wallet with a matching function selector. The hook runs in the wallet's storage context with full write access. If a hook implementation does not implement its own caller authentication, any external party can trigger state changes in the wallet's storage through the hook.
threats.investigation_rationale: While hook installation requires onlySelf authorization, the actual invocation has no access control. This creates a trust boundary violation where the hook's security depends entirely on the hook implementation's own access control, which is not enforced or verified by the wallet.

proof_of_signal.pattern: fallback() external payable { ... (bool success, bytes memory result) = target.delegatecall(msg.data); ... } — no msg.sender check before delegatecall; any caller triggers hook execution in wallet context

preconditions: Wallet has at least one hook registered via addHook
Hook implementation does not enforce msg.sender access control
Attacker knows the registered hook selector

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External caller invokes wallet fallback with hook selector, triggering delegatecall to hook without authentication
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner must have installed a vulnerable hook
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While hook is registered and external caller knows selector

operational_questions: What hook implementations are commonly deployed with Sequence wallets?
Do all hook implementations enforce their own access control?
Can hooks modify critical storage slots (imageHash, implementation, nonces)?
validation_requirements: Register a hook that modifies wallet storage without caller checks
Call wallet from external address with hook selector
Verify hook executes in wallet context and modifies storage

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify wallets with registered hooks by monitoring DefinedHook events
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Analyze hook implementation for missing access control
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call wallet with hook selector to trigger delegatecall
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Hook modifies wallet storage (implementation, imageHash, etc.) giving attacker control
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — direct on-chain attack if vulnerable hook exists

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full wallet takeover if hook can write to implementation or imageHash slots
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: May require implementation upgrade if storage is corrupted
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All wallet functions if critical storage is modified

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Smart contract analysis to identify vulnerable hooks
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time while vulnerable hook is registered

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: DefinedHook events followed by unexpected delegatecall executions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Wallet storage changes from non-self callers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: External addresses calling wallet with non-standard function selectors

threats.title: SessionManager Allows incrementUsageLimit Call at Index 0 Without Full Permission Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: In ExplicitSessionManager._validateExplicitCall, when call.to == address(this) (SessionManager) and callIdx == 0, the function returns early without validating any permission. The function only checks that value is 0 and returns the unchanged sessionUsageLimits. While _validateLimitUsageIncrement later validates the call data hash matches expected increments, the initial permission bypass means the first call to SessionManager is not checked against any signer permission configuration. Combined with the fact that incrementUsageLimit is externally callable and only enforces monotonic increase, the overall limit tracking depends entirely on the data hash match.
threats.investigation_rationale: The permission validation skip for self-calls at index 0 creates a control flow gap. If the data hash validation in _validateLimitUsageIncrement has any weakness or if the expected data construction diverges from actual execution, usage limits could be manipulated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (call.to == address(this)) { if (callIdx != 0) revert; if (call.value > 0) revert; return sessionUsageLimits; } — returns without permission validation for self-call at index 0

preconditions: Session key has any explicit permissions configured
First call in payload targets the SessionManager contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First call to SessionManager skips all permission checks; relies solely on data hash match for integrity
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any session key holder can structure payload this way
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any session transaction with usage limits

operational_questions: Is the data hash match in _validateLimitUsageIncrement sufficient to prevent manipulation?
Can a session key craft a payload where the expected data hash matches unexpected increments?
Are there edge cases where sessionUsageLimits is empty but a self-call is still present?
validation_requirements: Verify data hash construction cannot be manipulated
Test edge cases with empty usage limits and self-calls
Confirm no other SessionManager functions can be called at index 0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Session key crafts payload with first call targeting SessionManager
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Permission validation is skipped for this call
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attempt to construct incrementUsageLimit data that passes hash check but manipulates limits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If successful, usage limits are improperly set, allowing excess operations

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Usage limit tracking may be circumvented
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Session key exceeds intended operation limits
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Explicit session operations with cumulative limits

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of ABI encoding and hash collision

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: First calls in session transactions targeting SessionManager with unusual data
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Usage limits not tracking correctly across transactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Session keys exceeding their configured limits

threats.title: Blacklist Sort Validation Allows Duplicate Addresses Weakening Implicit Session Controls
threats.hypothesis_description: In SessionSig.recoverConfiguration, the blacklist sort validation uses strict less-than (if sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress) instead of less-than-or-equal. This allows duplicate consecutive addresses to pass validation. While duplicates don't break binary search correctness, they waste configuration space and could be exploited to reduce the effective blacklist size. More critically, the blacklist hash includes duplicates, meaning the imageHash depends on the exact blacklist encoding including duplicates, potentially allowing a session configuration with a seemingly-full blacklist that actually has fewer unique entries than expected.
threats.investigation_rationale: The blacklist is a security control for implicit sessions. If the wallet owner thinks they've blacklisted N addresses but duplicates reduce the effective count, some addresses may not be blacklisted as intended.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress) { revert SessionErrors.InvalidBlacklistUnsorted(); } — uses < instead of <= allowing duplicate addresses

preconditions: Session configuration includes implicit session support with blacklist
Blacklist is constructed with duplicate addresses

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Duplicate addresses pass blacklist sort validation, reducing effective blacklist coverage

operational_questions: Can duplicates cause the binary search to miss addresses that should be blacklisted?
Does the blacklist hash inclusion of duplicates create imageHash ambiguity?
Are there off-chain tools that prevent duplicate blacklist entries?
validation_requirements: Create blacklist with duplicates and verify binary search still works correctly
Test if duplicates reduce effective blacklist coverage
Verify imageHash is different with/without duplicates

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Construct session config where blacklist has duplicate entries filling slots
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Some intended blacklist addresses are omitted due to slot waste
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Implicit session calls to non-blacklisted addresses succeed
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Session key performs unauthorized implicit operations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Implicit calls to addresses that should be blacklisted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Session configs with duplicate blacklist entries
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Implicit calls succeeding to addresses intended for blacklisting

threats.title: ERC4337 validateUserOp Deposits ETH Before Signature Validation Allowing Deposit Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC4337v07.validateUserOp, the wallet transfers missingAccountFunds to the EntryPoint BEFORE validating the signature. While the EntryPoint manages deposits correctly, if the EntryPoint is compromised or has a vulnerability, the wallet's ETH could be drained via repeated validateUserOp calls with invalid signatures and non-zero missingAccountFunds. Even without EntryPoint compromise, the ordering means the wallet sends ETH for operations that may fail validation, though the EntryPoint's own accounting would handle refunds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deposit-before-validate ordering is standard for ERC4337 but creates a dependency on the EntryPoint's correctness. If the EntryPoint or its accounting has issues, the wallet's balance could be affected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (missingAccountFunds != 0) { IEntryPoint(entrypoint).depositTo{value: missingAccountFunds}(address(this)); } — ETH sent before this.isValidSignature check

preconditions: ERC4337 is enabled (entrypoint != address(0))
EntryPoint is trusted but may have accounting inconsistencies
Wallet has ETH balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH deposited to EntryPoint before signature is validated
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: EntryPoint is immutable and trusted infrastructure

operational_questions: Does the EntryPoint properly refund deposits for failed validations?
Can the deposit ordering be exploited to drain wallet ETH?
Is this ordering required by the ERC4337 specification?
validation_requirements: Submit UserOp with invalid signature and non-zero missingAccountFunds
Verify EntryPoint accounting handles the deposit correctly
Check if wallet balance is affected by failed validations

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify wallet with ERC4337 enabled and ETH balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit UserOp with invalid signature via compromised/malicious bundler
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wallet deposits ETH to EntryPoint before detecting invalid signature
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If EntryPoint has accounting issue, wallet loses ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires compromised EntryPoint or bundler — LOW realism

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Wallet ETH balance reduction from unnecessary deposits
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Would need to withdraw from EntryPoint

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Deep understanding of ERC4337 deposit mechanics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated failed validateUserOp calls with ETH deposits
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Wallet balance decreasing without successful transactions

threats.title: Implementation Storage Slot Collision Risk With Custom Proxy Pattern
threats.hypothesis_description: The Wallet proxy stores the implementation address at storage slot keyed by address(this) via sstore(address(), _imp). This is NOT an EIP-1967 compliant storage slot. Any module, hook, or inherited contract that accidentally writes to the storage slot equal to the wallet's own address would overwrite the implementation pointer, effectively bricking or hijacking the wallet. Since hooks execute via delegatecall in the wallet's context, a hook that writes to storage slot = address(wallet) would corrupt the proxy.
threats.investigation_rationale: Non-standard storage slot for implementation creates collision risk with any contract code running in the wallet's context. The address() value as a storage key is unusual and could conflict with mappings or arrays whose computed slot coincidentally matches.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function _setImplementation(address _imp) internal { assembly { sstore(address(), _imp) } } — uses address() as storage key; non-EIP-1967 slot

preconditions: A hook or delegatecall target writes to the storage slot matching the wallet's address
The wallet's address as a uint256 coincides with a computed mapping/array storage slot

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Non-standard implementation storage slot at address() is vulnerable to collision from delegatecall'd code
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner installs hook that writes to colliding slot
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When hook or delegatecall target modifies the colliding storage slot

operational_questions: Are there known hook implementations that write to storage slots that could collide?
Is the probability of keccak256(key) == address(wallet) negligible?
Could a malicious hook intentionally write to address() slot?
validation_requirements: Calculate probability of storage slot collision with address(this)
Test hook that writes to address() slot and verify implementation pointer corruption
Review all delegatecall targets for storage writes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify or create a hook that writes to storage slot = address(wallet)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Install the hook via wallet's addHook function
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger the hook to overwrite implementation pointer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Wallet proxy now delegates to attacker-controlled implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires installing malicious hook — LOW realism unless hook appears legitimate

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete wallet takeover via implementation redirect
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires external rescue mechanism (no standard recovery path)

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Deep understanding of EVM storage and delegatecall

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ImplementationUpdated events not preceded by updateImplementation calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Wallet suddenly delegating to unexpected implementation
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Hook installations followed by implementation changes
